---
title: "Track: Data Workflow in R"
description: "A practical learning path through the core data workflow every analyst needs: importing data, cleaning and reshaping it with the tidyverse, visualising it with ggplot2, and documenting work in Quarto."
author: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
date: 2026-06-16
date-modified: 2026-06-16
image: ../assets/images/placeholder.png

categories:
  - R
  - data-cleaning
  - visualization
  - tidyverse
  - quarto
  - reproducibility
  - BA

level:
  - BA

listing:
  - id: related-workflow-resources
    contents: "../resources/r-programming/*.qmd"
    include:
      categories: [data-cleaning, visualization, tidyverse]
    type: table
    sort: "date asc"
    fields: [title, description]

citation:
  type: entry
  container-title: "Methods Hub"
  title: "Track: Data Workflow in R"
  editor:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  url: https://euf-methodology-hub.netlify.app/tracks/data-workflow-in-r
---

# Who This Track Is For

This track is for students who have the R basics in place — you can write simple code, understand functions and vectors, and have an RStudio project set up — and now want to do something useful with data. It covers the end-to-end workflow from raw data files to a communicable result.

If you are not yet comfortable with R basics, start with the [Getting Started with R](getting-started-with-r.qmd) track first.

By the end of this track you will be able to:

- Import data from CSV, Excel, and Stata files into R
- Clean, filter, reshape, and summarise a dataset using `dplyr` and `tidyr`
- Build informative plots with `ggplot2` and save them to files
- Combine prose, code, and output in a reproducible Quarto document

---

# Core Sequence

## 1. Importing and Exporting Data

[Importing and Exporting Data in R](../resources/r-programming/importing-exporting-data.qmd)

Read CSV, RDS, Excel, and Stata files into R using `data.table`, `dplyr`, and `haven`; write data back to disk; and understand why relative file paths matter for reproducibility. The starting point for any real data project.

## 2. Data Preparation with the Tidyverse

[Data Preparation with the Tidyverse](../resources/r-programming/data-preparation-tidyverse.qmd)

Filter rows, select and rename columns, create new variables with `mutate()`, summarise by group, reshape between wide and long format with `tidyr`, and join tables. The tidyverse grammar covers 90% of data preparation tasks.

## 3. Visualisation with ggplot2

[Visualization with ggplot2](../resources/r-programming/visualization-ggplot2.qmd)

Build scatter plots, line plots, and bar charts using ggplot2's layer-by-layer grammar; map variables to aesthetics; adjust axis labels, colour palettes, and themes; and save finished plots with `ggsave()`. Visualisation is both an exploratory and a communication tool.

## 4. Reproducible Documents with Quarto

[Getting Started with Quarto](../resources/r-programming/getting-started-with-quarto.qmd)

Combine prose, R code, and output in a single `.qmd` file and render it to HTML or PDF. Quarto makes your analysis self-documenting: anyone who has the file can reproduce every number and figure in your report.

---

# Going Further

Once you have the data workflow in place, the natural next step is analysis. The [Introduction to Regression in R](introduction-to-regression-in-r.qmd) track picks up directly from here and covers fitting, interpreting, and diagnosing regression models. For producing polished, publication-ready figures, see [Publication-Ready Figures with ggplot2](../resources/r-programming/publication-ready-figures-ggplot2.qmd) (MA level).

---

# Related Resources

::: {#related-workflow-resources}
:::
